g.data-narration: Welcome to Module 3 on Image Restoration and Reconstruction
Image restoration objectively recovers original images from degraded versions using mathematical models of the degradation process.
g.text: Restoration is an objective process that uses a mathematical model of degradation to
recover the original, undegraded image. Unlike enhancement (which is subjective),
restoration aims to reverse known degradations like blur and noise.

g.data-narration: Unlike enhancement which makes images look better subjectively, restoration mathematically models and reverses specific degradation types.
The fundamental degradation model states that observed image g equals original f convolved with blur kernel h, plus additive noise.
g.text: Where * denotes convolution, h(x,y) is the Point Spread Function

g.data-narration: In the frequency domain, convolution becomes multiplication. The degraded image's Fourier transform equals the original times the optical transfer function plus noise.
Different noise types require different restoration approaches. Gaussian noise follows a normal distribution while salt-and-pepper appears as random black and white pixels.
Inverse filtering simply divides the degraded image's Fourier transform by the blur function to recover the original in theory.
g.text: Simply inverts the blur. Theoretically perfect if no noise, but...

g.data-narration: The critical flaw: inverse filtering severely amplifies noise wherever H is small or zero, making noise dominate the restored image catastrophically.
g.text: Small H values in frequency domain

g.data-narration: The Wiener filter solves the noise amplification problem by balancing deblurring with noise suppression based on the noise-to-signal power ratio.
g.text: The Wiener filter minimizes mean square error between original and restored image.
It acts as a band-pass filter—inverts H where signal is strong, suppresses where noise dominates.
Prevents noise explosion by adaptive filtering

g.data-narration: The Wiener filter formula divides by H multiplied by a ratio that balances signal restoration against noise suppression based on the power spectral ratio K.
g.text: Where K ≈ S_η(u,v) / S_f(u,v) is the noise-to-signal power ratio

g.data-narration: Constrained least squares requires only noise mean and variance, minimizing smoothness while satisfying a noise constraint for stable restoration.
g.text: Requires only noise mean and variance (much simpler than Wiener which needs full power spectra).
Finds solution that minimizes a smoothness operator (e.g., Laplacian) subject to constraint:

g.data-narration: Image reconstruction builds 2D images from 1D projections, essential for CT scans and other medical imaging modalities.
g.text: Used in CT scans, MRI, astronomy.

g.data-narration: The Radon transform computes line integrals of an image along various angles, representing projections as seen by CT scanners.
The Fourier Slice Theorem states that the Fourier transform of a projection at angle theta equals a slice through the 2D Fourier transform at the same angle.
Filtered back-projection filters projections with a ramp filter then smears them back across the image, summing all angles to reconstruct the final image.
g.text: f(x,y) = ∫ P_θ(s) * h(s - x cosθ - y sinθ) dθ

g.data-narration: To summarize: restoration uses degradation models to reverse blur and noise, with Wiener filtering as the optimal approach, while CT reconstruction uses the Fourier Slice Theorem.
